definition: A right of first refusal (ROFR) gives the holder the right to match any offer that the other party receives from a third party before that party can accept the third-party offer. If a shareholder wants to sell their shares, they must first offer those shares to the ROFR holders (usually the company and/or other shareholders) at the same price and on the same terms as the outside offer. Only if the ROFR holders decline can the seller proceed with the third party.
formalDefinition: A contractual pre-emption right entitling the holder to purchase shares or assets on the same terms and conditions offered by a bona fide third party, exercisable within a specified notice period following delivery of a transfer notice.
whenYouSeeThis: ROFRs appear in shareholders agreements, investors' rights agreements, co-sale agreements, and employment agreements where shares may be subject to vesting. They are standard in Canadian venture-backed companies and almost always paired with co-sale rights (tag-along) and drag-along provisions.
founderNote: A ROFR is a liquidity friction mechanism — it slows down a founder's ability to sell shares to outside parties. Venture investors use it to prevent strategic buyers from accumulating a stake without going through the existing shareholders. As a founder, understand the exercise period (usually 15–30 days) and whether the company or the other shareholders exercise first.
